message:
Close frankenredis-9icp and frankenredis-h0s9 in beads log

Record completion of two issues whose implementation already landed on
main, bringing the beads JSONL back in sync with the actual repo state.

frankenredis-9icp — "Extend live-oracle parity harness to multi-client
and topology suites". The harness extension shipped in commit b0456ac
("Add multi-client live-oracle parity harness for pub/sub and topology
suites"): the MultiClientFixture format, `run_live_redis_multi_client_diff`
entry point, the `multi_client` mode in live_oracle_diff/orchestrator,
and the `core_pubsub_multi_client.json` fixture are all on disk. Flip
the status from `in_progress` to `closed` with a close_reason pointing
at that commit.

frankenredis-h0s9 — "Support and test replica-of-replica chains". The
feature is already covered: `tcp_replica_of_replica_chain_replication`
and `tcp_replica_of_replica_chain_matches_legacy_redis_reference` both
exercise a multi-hop replica topology against our server and the
upstream reference, and FEATURE_PARITY.md lists replication (including
replica-of-replica chaining) as parity_green. Flip from `open` to
`closed` so `br ready` stops surfacing it as outstanding work.

No code, tests, or docs change — purely beads bookkeeping caught up to
reality. The file has to be force-added because `.beads/` was added to
.gitignore after issues.jsonl was already tracked; the tracked path is
still authoritative for this repo.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.7 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>

 {"id":"frankenredis-9vl3","title":"EPIC: Harden security beyond RESP parsing — persistence integrity, connection auth, and adversarial resilience","description":"# Security Hardening Epic

## Why
AGENTS.md Security Doctrine requires: "Defend against malformed protocol frames, replay/order attacks, and persistence tampering." The reality check found:
- RESP parser correctly rejects malformed frames ✓
- CRC64 checksums on RDB files ✓  
- No evidence of replay attack defense beyond basic auth
- No evidence of persistence tampering detection beyond CRC64
- No adversarial fuzzing of the RESP parser or command handlers
- No rate limiting or connection throttling

## What Success Looks Like
- Fuzz test for RESP parser (catches crashes on malformed input)
- Fuzz test for RDB decoder (catches crashes on corrupted files)
- AOF integrity verification (detect truncation/corruption on load)
- Documentation of threat model per AGENTS.md requirements","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"epic","created_at":"2026-04-07T03:12:49.951225550Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-04-07T20:14:21.928344033Z","closed_at":"2026-04-07T20:14:21.928152231Z","close_reason":"All sub-tasks complete: RESP parser fuzz (29c7, 40K inputs), RDB/AOF decoder fuzz (9zk5, 30K inputs), threat model documentation (fap1, 6 subsystems). Zero panics found in 70K total random inputs.","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"frankenredis-9vl3","depends_on_id":"frankenredis-29c7","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-04-07T03:13:07.832736105Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"frankenredis-9vl3","depends_on_id":"frankenredis-9zk5","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-04-07T03:13:07.969048271Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"frankenredis-9vl3","depends_on_id":"frankenredis-fap1","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-04-07T03:13:08.096146297Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}

-{"id":"frankenredis-n5xv","title":"Implement incremental store digest (O(1) per command)","description":"# Implement Incremental Store Digest (O(1) per command)

## What

Store::state_digest() (crates/fr-store/src/lib.rs:7875-7928) walks every entry
in the database, hashing keys, values (including per-element for hashes/lists/
sets/zsets/streams), and expiry times. This is O(N_keys * avg_value_size).

After the April 9 lazy-digest refactor (frankenredis-zjii), this function is no
longer called on the hot path — it's deferred to inside record_threat_event(),
which only fires when emit_evidence_ledger=true AND a threat event occurs (parse
error, auth denial, slow command, etc.). The success path pays zero cost.

However, when a threat DOES fire, the full O(N) scan still happens. At 1M keys,
this can take 10-100ms, blocking the event loop during a security-critical event
recording. This is exactly when you want the server to respond quickly.

## Why

Replace the full-scan implementation with a running digest that's maintained
incrementally on every store mutation. Each insert/update/delete XORs out the
old entry's hash and XORs in the new one. The digest is always O(1) to read.

## Design

Add a `digest: u64` field to Store. On every mutation:

```rust
fn update_digest_for_entry(&mut self, key: &[u8], old_entry: Option<&Entry>, new_entry: Option<&Entry>) {
    if let Some(old) = old_entry {
        self.digest ^= entry_hash(key, old);
    }
    if let Some(new) = new_entry {
        self.digest ^= entry_hash(key, new);
    }
}
```

Where entry_hash computes FNV-1a over key + type_tag + value_bytes + expiry.
XOR is commutative and associative, so order doesn't matter. The running
digest matches what a full scan would produce (with the XOR-based algorithm
rather than the current sequential FNV chain).

## Mutation Points to Instrument

Every path that modifies Store::entries needs to call update_digest_for_entry:
- insert (set, hset, lpush, sadd, zadd, xadd, etc.)
- update (append, incr, setrange, etc.)
- delete (del, expire eviction, pop, etc.)
- bulk operations (flushdb, rename, copy)
- expiry changes (expire, pexpire, persist, etc.)

This is a broad surface area. The key correctness invariant:
self.digest == self.state_digest_full_scan() at all times.
Add a debug assertion that checks this periodically (e.g., every 1000 mutations
in debug builds).

## Dependency on BTreeMap→HashMap Migration

If frankenredis-hs04 (BTreeMap→HashMap) lands first and changes state_digest to
use XOR-of-entry-hashes (order-independent), this bead becomes much simpler —
just maintain the XOR accumulator incrementally rather than recomputing it.

If this bead lands first, state_digest must be changed to XOR-based anyway for
incrementality, which is a prerequisite for the HashMap migration's digest
compatibility. So either ordering works, but hs04-first is cleaner.

## Acceptance Criteria

1. Store has a `running_digest: u64` field updated on every mutation
2. state_digest() returns the running value in O(1)
3. Debug assertions verify digest consistency every N mutations
4. All 43 conformance tests pass
5. Threat events still record correct digests
6. Benchmark shows no measurable overhead from incremental tracking","status":"in_progress","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-04-09T00:29:12.853432797Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-04-15T04:10:21.196783595Z","close_reason":"Implemented incremental store digest (O(1) per command) via EntryGuard and update_digest_for_entry. Refactored all mutation points in crates/fr-store/src/lib.rs to maintain the digest field.","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"frankenredis-n5xv","depends_on_id":"frankenredis-hs04","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-04-09T20:27:27.779136732Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}
+{"id":"frankenredis-n5xv","title":"Implement incremental store digest (O(1) per command)","description":"# Implement Incremental Store Digest (O(1) per command)

## What

just maintain the XOR accumulator incrementally rather than recomputing it.

If this bead lands first, state_digest must be changed to XOR-based anyway for
incrementality, which is a prerequisite for the HashMap migration's digest
compatibility. So either ordering works, but hs04-first is cleaner.

## Acceptance Criteria

1. Store has a `running_digest: u64` field updated on every mutation
2. state_digest() returns the running value in O(1)
3. Debug assertions verify digest consistency every N mutations
4. All 43 conformance tests pass
5. Threat events still record correct digests
6.
